questions.text: What should you do before opening your first Opik pull request?
options: Sign the Contributor License Agreement (CLA)
Open a draft PR with no linked issue
Skip local tests if CI looks green later
Push directly to main on comet-ml/opik
questions.explanation: Opik requires the CLA so maintainers can accept your contribution. Open and sign it before your first PR.

questions.text: How should a pull request connect to the GitHub issue it fixes?
options: Mention the issue only in a Slack message
Use Fixes #... or Resolves #... in the PR description
Put the issue number only in the branch name
Close the issue manually before opening the PR
questions.explanation: Linking with Fixes #... or Resolves #... ties the PR to the tracked issue and helps maintainers review and close work correctly.

questions.text: What role do GitHub Actions play in Opik contribution?
options: They replace code review entirely
They are continuous integration checks in the software development lifecycle
They only run after a PR is merged
They host the Opik production UI
questions.explanation: GitHub Actions workflows under .github/workflows/ run CI checks on pull requests as part of Opik's SDLC.

questions.text: When you pick an issue, where should most of your changes land?
options: Across every SDK and the backend in one PR
In the component area the issue targets
Only in this onboarding-tool repository
In deployment/ for every bugfix
questions.explanation: Stay in the component area your issue targets so the PR stays reviewable and matches the right guide and CI workflows.

questions.text: If you use AI while contributing, who is accountable for correctness, licensing, and security?
options: The AI model vendor
GitHub Actions alone
You, the human author
Whoever merges the PR without reading it
questions.explanation: AI assistance is welcome, but a human author stays accountable for correctness, licensing, and security. Run local checks before you open the PR.
